questions.text: The point at which an applied force produces linear motion but no rotatory Motion is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The term "mid-point" typically refers to the halfway point between two ends of an object or a line. While it may have certain properties, it is not commonly associated with the concept of producing specific types of motion due to applied forces.

The optical center is a term used in the context of optics, particularly in lenses and mirrors. It relates to the path of light passing through a lens without deviating. While it has significance in optics, it is not typically used to describe the motion of objects due to applied forces.

In general physics, "pole" can refer to various points or regions depending on the context. It may refer to the magnetic poles of a magnet or other specific points in different physical scenarios. However, "pole" is not commonly used to describe the point at which an applied force produces specific types of motion.

questions.explanation: The point at which an applied force produces linear motion but no rotational motion is the "center of gravity." The center of gravity is the point within an object or system where the weight is evenly distributed in all directions. When an applied force is directed through the center of gravity, it results in pure linear motion without causing the object to rotate.When an external force is applied to an object, it causes translational (linear) motion when applied through the center of gravity. However, if the force is applied away from the center of gravity, it can create both linear motion and rotational motion, causing the object to pivot or rotate.The center of gravity is a critical concept in physics, particularly in mechanics and the study of equilibrium. It plays a role in determining stability, balance, and the response of an object to applied forces.

questions.text: A person is standing on a bus. When the bus starts moving forward suddenly:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A person standing in a bus moves backward when the bus moves forward. When the bus moves forward, the lower part of his body begins to move along with the bus while the upper part of his body continues to remain at rest due to inertia. That is why a person falls backward when the bus starts.

A person standing in a bus moves backward when the bus moves forward. When the bus moves forward, the lower part of his body begins to move along with the bus while the upper part of his body continues to remain at rest due to inertia. That is why a person falls backward when the bus starts.

A person standing in a bus moves backward when the bus moves forward. When the bus moves forward, the lower part of his body begins to move along with the bus while the upper part of his body continues to remain at rest due to inertia. That is why a person falls backward when the bus starts.

The following is the solution: When the bus starts moving forward suddenly, the person inside the bus will also move backward. This is due to inertia, one of the fundamental principles of physics. Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. When the bus accelerates forward, the person inside the bus is initially at rest or moving at the same speed as the bus. As a result, the person will tend to stay at rest or move at the same initial speed, which makes them appear to move backward to oppose the direction relative to the bus. This is why the person moves backward when the bus accelerates suddenly.

Also, according to Newton's second law, the force is equal to mass times acceleration. As such, the train hits the fly with a massive amount of force, equal to the acceleration times the mass of the train. questions.text: If the velocity of the body is decreasing, the acceleration is negative. The negative acceleration is known as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Retardation is the negative acceleration of a body. When the velocity of a body is decreasing, it means that the body is slowing down or moving in the opposite direction of its initial motion. Retardation is essentially a negative acceleration, and it is used to describe situations where an object is decelerating or slowing down.

Deceleration is another term used to describe the situation when an object's velocity is decreasing. It is synonymous with negative acceleration. When an object decelerates, it means it is slowing down.

Displacement is a vector quantity that represents the change in position of an object, and it can be positive or negative depending on the direction of motion. However, the term "negative displacement" is not directly related to the acceleration of the body.

The term "negative motion" is not commonly used in physics or kinematics. The correct terminology to describe a decreasing velocity is "deceleration" or "retardation," which both refer to negative acceleration.

questions.text: For a straight trajectory of a particle instantaneous velocity is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The average velocity is calculated by dividing the total displacement of an object by the total time taken to cover that displacement. It represents the overall change in position over a given time interval. On the other hand, instantaneous velocity refers to the velocity of an object at an exact moment in time. It represents the velocity of the object at a specific point along its trajectory. The average velocity considers the total displacement and total time, giving an average value over that interval. It does not account for any changes or variations in velocity that may occur during that interval. The instantaneous velocity, on the other hand, represents the velocity of the object at a precise moment in time. It captures any changes in velocity that may have occurred during that specific instant. Therefore, stating that the instantaneous velocity is equal to 2 times the average velocity is incorrect. The average velocity gives information about the overall change in position over a time interval, while the instantaneous velocity specifically refers to the velocity at a particular instant in time. They are distinct concepts and are not directly proportional.

For a straight trajectory of a particle, the instantaneous velocity remains constant in both magnitude and direction. Velocity is defined as the rate of change of displacement with respect to time. In a straight trajectory, the displacement of the particle changes linearly with time. Since the displacement is changing at a constant rate, the particle's velocity remains constant. The magnitude of the instantaneous velocity represents the speed of the particle, which remains constant in a straight trajectory. The direction of the instantaneous velocity also remains constant since the particle is moving along a straight line without changing its orientation. In summary, for a straight trajectory of a particle, the instantaneous velocity remains constant in both magnitude and direction.

For a particle moving along a straight trajectory, the instantaneous velocity refers to the velocity of the particle at a specific point in time. It represents the magnitude and direction of the particle's motion at that particular moment. If the particle is in motion along a straight line, its instantaneous velocity will generally have a non-zero value unless it comes to a complete stop or changes direction. The magnitude of the instantaneous velocity indicates the speed at which the particle is moving, while the direction represents the direction of its motion along the straight trajectory. In situations where the particle is at rest (not moving), the instantaneous velocity would indeed be zero since there is no motion. However, if the particle is moving along a straight trajectory, the instantaneous velocity will typically have a non-zero value.

questions.text: Displacement of sun with respect to earth is?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The displacement of the Sun with respect to Earth can be described in two different ways:1. Daily Apparent Motion: The Sun appears to move across the sky during the day due to Earth's rotation on its axis. This apparent motion is from east to west, and it takes approximately 24 hours for the Sun to complete one full circuit around the sky. This displacement of the Sun is a result of Earth's rotation and is responsible for the day-night cycle.2. Annual Apparent Motion: Over the course of a year, the Sun's position in the sky changes due to Earth's orbital motion around the Sun. The Earth's orbit around the Sun is not a perfect circle but an ellipse. Therefore, the distance between Earth and the Sun changes slightly throughout the year. This causes the Sun's apparent position against the background stars to shift gradually, resulting in the changing seasons.During the year, Earth's axial tilt also plays a crucial role. As the Earth orbits the Sun, the tilt of its axis causes the Sun's rays to strike different latitudes at varying angles, leading to the changing seasons. For example, when the Northern Hemisphere is tilted towards the Sun, it experiences summer, while the Southern Hemisphere experiences winter. Six months later, the situation is reversed.It's important to note that while we perceive the Sun's motion as displacement from the Earth's perspective, in reality, the Sun is stationary in its position, and the apparent motion is a result of Earth's rotation and orbital motion.

questions.text: A car travels 30 m toward east, then it takes turn and travels 40 m towards north. It takes 50 seconds. Its average velocity is: ....

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.

To calculate average velocity, we need to divide the total displacement by the total time taken. The car travels 30 m toward the east, and then it turns and travels 40 m toward the north. Since these displacements are at right angles to each other, we can find the total displacement using the Pythagorean theorem. Total displacement = √(302 + 402) = √(900 + 1600) = √2500 = 50 m The total time taken is given as 50 seconds. Average velocity = Total displacement / Total time taken = 50 m / 50 s = 1 m/s Therefore, the average velocity of the car is 1 m/s.

Newton's first law of motion is applicable only in inertial frames of reference. An inertial frame of reference is a frame of reference in which Newton's laws of motion are valid. In an inertial frame of reference, an object will remain at rest or in motion at a constant velocity unless acted upon by a force. The Earth is not an inertial frame of reference because it is rotating around the Sun. This rotation causes the Earth to experience a centrifugal force, which is a force that pushes objects away from the center of rotation. This force is what causes objects to move in a curved path around the Earth. However, the Earth's rotation is very slow, so the centrifugal force is very small. For most purposes, the Earth can be treated as an inertial frame of reference.

questions.explanation: This is a case of inelastic collision. Let us consider the truck with mass 'm' is coming from the left with velocity '2v' ⇒ The momentum of the truck coming from left = m × 2v = 2mv Let us consider the truck with mass '3m' is coming from the right side with veloctiy 'v' ⇒ The momentum of the truck coming from right = 3m × (-v) The negative sign should be considered because the truck coming from the right is moving in the opposite direction to that of truck coming from the left. Total momentum of Railway truck system before collision = 2mv - 3mv = -mv Now let us calculate the momentum of this system after the collision : As both the truck's stick together after the collision, the total mass of the Railway truck system = m + 3m = 4m But we don't know the velocity with which this combined truck system is moving. Let us consider the velocity of the combined truck system as : v(don't know) Momentum of this combined truck system = 4m × v(don't know) Momentum before collision = Momentum after collision ⇒ mv = 4m × v(don't know) ⇒ v(don't know) = v/4 = 0.25v Hence the speed of the combined truck system after collison = 0.25v = 1/4v = v/4

questions.text: In the absence of air resistance, an object thrown into the air at an angle of 20 degrees to the ground. It will experience:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: a) Constant vertical acceleration: This option suggests that the object will experience a constant acceleration in the vertical direction. In the absence of air resistance, the only force acting on the object vertically is gravity. Gravity causes a constant acceleration of approximately 9.8 m/s² downward, regardless of the object's initial velocity or angle of projection. Therefore, this option is correct. The vertical acceleration is indeed constant and equal to the acceleration due to gravity.

b) Changing vertical acceleration: This option proposes that the vertical acceleration of the object changes over time. As mentioned above, in the absence of air resistance, the only force acting vertically is gravity, which provides a constant acceleration downward. Therefore, the vertical acceleration remains constant.

c) Changing horizontal acceleration: This option suggests that the horizontal acceleration of the object changes over time. However, in the absence of any horizontal forces (like air resistance or propulsion), the horizontal acceleration of the object remains zero. The object will continue moving horizontally with a constant velocity (which is determined by its initial velocity) while undergoing vertical acceleration due to gravity.

d) Constant (non-zero) horizontal acceleration: This option implies that the object experiences a non-zero horizontal acceleration. In the absence of any horizontal forces, the horizontal acceleration of the object is zero. The object's horizontal motion remains unchanged, with constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external horizontal force.

questions.explanation: The only acceleration a free-thrown object experiences is the gravitational acceleration, which at all times is constant (in this case) and is vertically towards the earth's surface. It will experience weightlessness, for a while, then a jolt, when it hits the ground (moves under the influence of gravitational acceleration) The angle is irrelevant: the experience will be the same regardless of the angle. The only difference that angle would make is how long the experience would last. Without the launch speed, the time that the experience lasts also remains unknown.

questions.text: If two equal forces act perpendicularly upon each other, then the angle between the resultant force and a horizontal surface will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the two forces are equal and act perpendicularly to each other, the resultant force cannot be zero degrees unless one of the forces is zero. This option is incorrect.
A 30-degree angle would only be possible if the two forces have different magnitudes. Since the question states that the forces are equal, this option is incorrect.

When two equal forces are applied perpendicularly to each other, they create a right angle. In such a case, the resultant force can be determined using the Pythagorean theorem. The magnitude of the resultant force is equal to the square root of the sum of the squares of the magnitudes of the two forces. Since the two forces are equal, their magnitudes are the same. Therefore, if we consider each force to have a magnitude of "F," the magnitude of the resultant force will be: Resultant force = √(F^2 + F^2) = √(2F^2) = √2F Now, when the resultant force is resolved into horizontal and vertical components, each component will have a magnitude of √2F. Since the vertical component is equal to the force acting vertically, the angle between the resultant force and the horizontal surface will be 45 degrees. Therefore, the correct option is C, 45 degrees.
A 60-degree angle would only be possible if the two forces have different magnitudes. Since the question states that the forces are equal, this option is incorrect.

The following is the solution: A rocket works on Newton's third law. Newton's third law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. In the case of a rocket, the action is the force of the exhaust gases being expelled out of the back of the rocket. The reaction is the force that propels the rocket forward.

Newton's gravitational law states that every object in the universe attracts every other object with a force that is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. This law does not apply to rockets, as the force of gravity is not the only force acting on a rocket. The force of the exhaust gases is also acting on the rocket, and this force is much larger than the force of gravity.

questions.text: A ball with original momentum +4.0 kg x m/s hits a wall and bounces straight back without losing any kinetic energy. The change in momentum of the ball is:

The following is the solution: When a ball bounces off a wall and changes direction without losing any kinetic energy, it experiences a change in momentum due to the reversal of its velocity. The change in momentum (Δp) of an object is given by the final momentum (pf) minus the initial momentum (pi): Δp = pf - pi where: pf = final momentum of the ball pi = initial momentum of the ball Given that the ball has an original momentum of +4.0 kg·m/s, and it bounces back without losing any kinetic energy, its final momentum (pf) will be -4.0 kg·m/s (opposite direction). Now, we can calculate the change in momentum: Δp = (-4.0 kg·m/s) - (+4.0 kg·m/s)Δp = -8.0 kg·m/s So, the change in momentum of the ball will be -8.0 kg·m/s. The negative sign indicates the momentum has reversed the direction during the collision with the wall.

Using the equation of motion for constant acceleration: S1 = ut + (1/2)at2 where: u = initial velocity (0 m/s), a = constant acceleration (10 m/s²) and, t = time (1 second) Substituting the values: S1 = 0 + (1/2) x 10 x (1)2 S1 = 0 + 5 S1 = 5 meters So, during the 1st second, the distance traveled (S1) is 5 meters. 2nd second: In the 2nd second, the body continues with the constant acceleration of 10 m/s². The distance traveled during this time interval is denoted by S2. Using the same equation of motion for constant acceleration: S2 = ut + (1/2)at2 where: u = initial velocity (0 m/s), a = constant acceleration (10 m/s²) and, t = time (2 seconds) Substituting the values: S2 = 0 + (1/2) x 10 x (2)2 S2 = 0 + 20 S2 = 20 meters So, during the 2nd second, the distance traveled (S2) is 20 meters. questions.text: A projectile is launched at 45° to the horizontal with a force of initial kinetic energy "E". Assuming air resistance to be negligible, what will be the kinetic energy of the project when it reaches its highest point?

options.explanation: The following is the solution: When a projectile is launched at an angle of 45 degrees to the horizontal, its horizontal and vertical components of velocity are equal. So, the horizontal velocity (Vx) of the projectile at the highest point is the same as the initial velocity (Vi) multiplied by the cosine of the launch angle: Vx = Vi cos(45 degrees) Since we are considering only the horizontal component of velocity at the highest point, the kinetic energy (K.E-highest) at the highest point can be calculated as follows: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x Vx2 Substituting the expression for Vx: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi cos(45degrees))2 Now, cos(45 degrees) is equal to √2/2: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi √2 /2)2 = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 (2 /4)) = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 / 2) Finally, since the initial kinetic energy (Ki) is given as "i," we can write the equation as: K.E-highest = Ki / 2 Therefore, the kinetic energy of the projectile at its highest point will be half of its initial kinetic energy, i.e., K.E-highest = Ki / 2 which can also be written as 0.5 Ki or as the question asked in terms of E, it will become 0.5 E, hence option A is correct.

options.explanation: When a body is moving in an inertial frame of reference, it means that there are no external forces acting on the body. In such a scenario, according to Newton's first law of motion, the body will continue to move with a constant velocity (including zero velocity if it was initially at rest) unless acted upon by an external force. Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity. If there are no external forces acting on the body, the velocity of the body will remain constant, and therefore, the rate of change of velocity (acceleration) will be zero. Therefore, the correct answer is A: zero acceleration, indicating that the body maintains a constant velocity in the absence of external forces.
